nativeRange.habitat: The Western Clawed Frog inhabits slow-moving streams, temporary pools, swamps, freshwater marshes, ponds, and ditches throughout subtropical and tropical lowland West Africa. During the dry season it has been reported to seek shelter in shallow streams, tucking beneath tree roots, stones, or holes in riverbanks.

decorPreferences: soft sand or fine smooth gravel substrate
driftwood and smooth rockwork for shelter

diet.notes: Generally accepts earthworms, bloodworms, blackworms, brine shrimp, raw squid, and sinking pellets formulated for clawed frogs or carnivorous fish. Adults have been reported to take small live or frozen prey readily. Feed sparingly two to three times per week to prevent obesity; juveniles need more frequent small meals. Tadpoles are filter-feeders consuming zooplankton in the wild.

goodWith: Larger peaceful fish too big to be swallowed
avoidWith: African Clawed Frogs (Xenopus laevis) due to outcompetition and chytrid cross-transmission risk

summary: The Western Clawed Frog is a small, fully aquatic frog from West Africa, reaching 4 to 6 cm as an adult. A close relative of the African Clawed Frog, it tends to prey on small tankmates and requires a tight-fitting lid to prevent escape.
careNotes:
The Western Clawed Frog (Xenopus tropicalis) is the smaller, warmer-water relative of the better-known African Clawed Frog (Xenopus laevis). It is less commonly encountered in the home-aquarium trade than X. laevis, though it does appear through specialist suppliers. The species is sometimes listed under its older genus name Silurana tropicalis, and that older name remains in circulation in both hobby and scientific contexts. It can be distinguished from X. laevis by its smaller adult size and by having four claws on each hind foot rather than three.

Care requirements largely parallel those of X. laevis but at a smaller scale and warmer temperature: sources generally advise keeping water in the 24–28 °C range, as the cooler temperatures that X. laevis tolerates are not appropriate for this species. It is broadly considered tolerant of a wide pH and hardness range, though stable parameters around pH 6.5–7.8 are typically recommended.

A 75 L footprint is a commonly cited minimum for a small group; a single specimen can be kept in less space. A tightly fitting cover is essential — these frogs have been reported to climb and escape from open tanks.

The Western Clawed Frog is a capable predator relative to its size and has been known to consume any fish or invertebrate small enough to fit in its mouth. It is generally best kept as a species-only group or alongside significantly larger, robust fish that cannot be swallowed and are not prone to fin-nipping.

Like all Xenopus, this species is a known carrier and reservoir of chytrid fungus (Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis, the organism responsible for the amphibian disease chytridiomycosis). New specimens should be quarantined, captive animals must never be released into the wild, and mixing with other amphibians is inadvisable without veterinary screening.

The skin of clawed frogs is permeable and sensitive to chlorine, chloramine, copper, and many fish medications. All water should be dechlorinated before use, copper-based treatments should be avoided entirely, and any medication should be researched for amphibian safety before it is added to the tank.
breedingNotes: Breeding has been achieved in captivity and is routine in laboratory settings. In research contexts spawning is commonly induced by hormone injection, but hobbyist breeding is generally reported as moderately difficult and relies on conditioning adults through a simulated dry season — lowering the water level and then performing a significant cool water change to mimic rainfall — combined with dim lighting and the provision of floating plants or other surfaces for egg attachment. Females produce large clutches of individually deposited eggs that stick to plants or drift freely in the water column. Generation time from egg to sexually mature adult is notably short, under five months under good conditions. Newly hatched tadpoles feed by filtering microscopic particles from the water and require appropriately sized suspended food rather than the sinking foods used for the adults.

amphibian.breedingNotes: Hormone-induced spawning is routine in research settings. Hobbyist spawning is generally reported as moderately difficult, typically requiring a simulation of seasonal drought followed by a cool water change, along with dim lighting and floating substrates for egg attachment. Generation time from egg to sexual maturity is under 5 months.
